aliases: Red rice wine chicken noodle soup

english: Red rice wine chicken noodle soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for red wine chicken meesua; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595). A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pasta and noodle dishes) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for meesua-based dishes in the current FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for traditional Singaporean noodle preparations.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 350g (one bowl), comprising meesua noodles, chicken pieces, radish, and broth with red rice wine.

nutrient_source.title: Red Wine Chicken Meesua — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Red Wine Chicken Meesua (cr_id F05060107). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6g/100g based on HPB compositional data for similar chicken-based noodle dishes; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — wheat vermicelli / thin wheat noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Red Wine Chicken Meesua as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on the GI of wheat vermicelli / thin wheat noodles (GI ~50–55) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The protein and fat content of the dish (6.3g each per 100g) further attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 22.05) / 100 ≈ 11.5 for a 350g serving (carbohydrate 6.3g/100g × 3.5 = 22.05g).

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 310mg/100g (approximately 1085mg per typical 350g serving) exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 800mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce and salt during preparation, and avoid consuming the full broth to limit sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat vermicelli carbohydrate load; low GI (~52) partially mitigated by protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a low estimated GI of approximately 52 and a moderate glycemic load of 11.5 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of meesua (refined wheat vermicelli) to avoid cumulative carbohydrate load, and note that residual alcohol from red rice wine may interact with diabetes medications and disrupt glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (310mg/100g) and moderate fat (2.2g/100g) with low saturated fat (0.6g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (0.6g/100g) and moderate overall fat profile are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the sodium level of 310mg/100g (approximately 1085mg per serving) is a significant concern for cardiovascular disease management and patients should request a reduced-sodium preparation or limit broth consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic) offset by elevated sodium and residual alcohol from red rice wine
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger (gingerols) and garlic (organosulfur compounds) in this dish provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to RA management; however, individuals on methotrexate or biologics should confirm with their rheumatologist whether trace cooking alcohol from red rice wine is acceptable, and sodium at 310mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Wine Chicken Meesua (红酒鸡面线; Mee Sua Ayam Arak Merah) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese noodle dish consisting of thin wheat vermicelli (meesua) simmered in a fragrant broth made with red rice wine (ang chow / 红糟), chicken pieces, radish, ginger, garlic, and sesame oil. The dish is a well-known postpartum confinement food in Chinese Singaporean culture, traditionally prepared to aid recovery after childbirth, though it is also consumed as a comforting everyday meal. The broth is rich, aromatic, and slightly sweet from the red rice wine, with a savoury depth from soy sauce and sesame oil. The meesua noodles are soft and silky, absorbing the flavourful broth. Nutritionally, the dish provides 70 kcal per 100g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of 6.3g protein, 2.2g fat, and 6.3g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 310mg/100g, making it a moderate concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 52 (low) reflects the wheat vermicelli base, partially attenuated by co-ingested protein and fat. The dish contains gluten (wheat meesua) and soy (soy sauce) as allergens and is not suitable for halal diets due to the red rice wine content. Clinically, the dish is suitable for general consumption in moderate portions but requires sodium management for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients, and carbohydrate monitoring for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060107) with a provenance confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (wheat vermicelli) in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source. Multilingual names are well-established for this traditional Singaporean Chinese dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6g/100g based on HPB compositional data for similar chicken-based noodle dishes; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB record for this specific entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean Chinese preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat vermicelli (GI ~52) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 4. The dish contains red rice wine (ang chow) and is therefore not halal; the input record incorrectly tagged 'halal' and 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' in dietary_tags — these have been corrected to false in dietary_flags. 5. The input record classified primary_cuisine as 'western' which is incorrect; this dish is a traditional Singaporean Chinese preparation and has been reclassified accordingly. 6. All core macros (protein_g 6.3, fat_g 2.2, carbohydrate_g 6.3) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.

Corrected dietary_flags: is_halal set to false (dish contains red rice wine), is_vegetarian set to false, is_vegan set to false.
Added saturated_fat_g (0.6) estimated from HPB compositional data for similar dishes.
Set all unavailable micronutrient fields to -1 with documented justification in notes.

Added all three required health_context conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with clinical specificity.
